title: Production of printed books per half century
subtitle: A book is defined as a published title with more than 49 pages.
note: Observations are plotted at the midpoint of the 50-year-period they refer to.
caption:
Production of printed books per half century

A book is defined as a published title with more than 49 pages.

This line chart tracks the production of printed books per half century from 1475 to 1775, with observations plotted at the midpoint of each 50-year period. The vertical axis measures volume in millions, ranging from 0 to 160 million. France, Great Britain, and Germany occupy the highest positions on the chart by the final data point in 1775. The line for France rises steeply in the latter half of the timeline, reaching the highest value near 160 million. Great Britain follows a similar upward trajectory, ending just below 140 million, while Germany concludes near 120 million. Italy displays a distinct pattern, rising to approximately 85 million around 1675 before dipping and recovering to roughly 75 million by 1775. The Netherlands shows steady growth throughout the period, finishing between 40 and 60 million. A cluster of lines representing Sweden, Ireland, Spain, Russia, Poland, Belgium, and Switzerland remains in the lower section of the chart, generally staying below the 20 million mark for most of the timeline before showing slight increases toward 1775. The category labeled "Other countries" sits slightly above this lower cluster, reaching approximately 40 million by the end of the period. The visual data indicates a significant divergence in production volumes among the top three countries compared to the rest of the listed regions over the three centuries shown.
